USD.catalyst: The U.S. dollar remains supported by a hawkish Federal Reserve regime under Chair Kevin Warsh, whose appointment has driven a rally in real yields and prompted global pension funds to unwind FX hedges. Geopolitical escalation in the Middle East, specifically the exchange of missile and drone strikes between U.S. and Iranian forces over the July 13 weekend, has fueled safe-haven demand and inflation fears following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. While a recent PPI miss has allowed for some tactical softening, the greenback maintains a dominant carry profile with SOFR at 3.63%, further bolstered by "Liberation Day" market unrest recovery flows. Investors are now focused on upcoming testimony from Chair Warsh and the broader impact of Iran's warnings that continued U.S. attacks will spread the conflict to new regional areas.

sources.title: Global pension funds pull back on FX hedges as US dollar woes ease - Forex Factory
Asian shares mostly decline with South Korea’s Kospi down 6.6%, while oil prices slip - AP News

Global pension funds pull back on FX hedges as dollar woes ease - KITCO

GBP.catalyst: Sterling is trading under pressure near 1.3525, breaking below the 1.3550 support level as the market reacts to U.S. strikes on Iran and the subsequent spike in oil prices. While Pantheon Macroeconomics suggests the UK economy is defying higher energy costs, May GDP data released this week showed a muted 0.1% month-on-month expansion, with services barely offsetting weakness in production and construction. Options markets are signaling heightened anxiety, with implied volatility at 11.0% representing a 4.7% premium over realized volatility as traders hedge against geopolitical tail risks. Despite a neutral OIS carry profile, the pound remains vulnerable to the dollar's safe-haven bid and the inflationary implications of the Hormuz closure.

sources.title: Pound Sterling: UK Economy Defies Higher Oil Prices, Says Pantheon - Exchange Rates UK
Dollar jumps on renewed Middle East attacks, Hormuz closure - CNBC
Sterling slips as Iran strikes push up oil prices and boost dollar - KITCO

EUR.catalyst: The Euro is currently testing the 1.1468 level against the dollar as markets digest a surprise miss in U.S. PPI data, though the single currency remains hampered by a significant -145bp OIS carry disadvantage against the greenback. Domestic fundamentals have softened following an unexpected fall in Eurozone industrial output, contributing to a negative Citigroup Economic Surprise Index reading of -14. Leveraged funds maintain a net short position of over 66,000 contracts according to recent COT data, while the options market is pricing a +2.0% volatility risk premium. Traders are monitoring the 1.1500 handle as a key topside target, though gains are capped by the broader flight to safety triggered by renewed Middle East hostilities.

sources.title: Pound Sterling: UK Economy Defies Higher Oil Prices, Says Pantheon - Exchange Rates UK
Dollar jumps on renewed Middle East attacks, Hormuz closure - CNBC

JPY.catalyst: The Yen continues to languish near 40-year lows around 162.11, driven by a massive +265bp OIS carry disadvantage against the U.S. dollar. Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama has attempted to stem the decline by unexpectedly urging the GPIF and domestic households to increase investments in Japanese financial assets, a move that briefly triggered G10 outperformance. However, market participants like Standard Chartered’s Steve Englander remain skeptical of coordinated U.S.-Japan intervention, noting that many traders are waiting for a dip to resume selling the currency. While a senior BOJ official warned that delaying rate adjustments could harm the economy, the 25d risk reversal at -1.39 indicates that while the spot remains weak, the options market is paying a high premium for JPY strength protection.

sources.title: Dollar debasement arguments are misplaced and U.S.-Japan FX coordination won't happen: Analyst - CNBC
Fragile yen set for weekly decline as intervention risks mount - CNBC
Morning Bid: Japan calling capital home - Reuters
Japanese Yen: Policy shift talk supports JPY – MUFG - Bitget

AUD.catalyst: The Australian Dollar is navigating conflicting forces as renewed conflict between the Trump administration and Iran pushes oil prices toward the $100 per barrel mark. Economists warn that if the Middle East airstrikes are not resolved within the week, the Reserve Bank of Australia may be forced into a fourth interest rate hike this year to combat resurgent fuel-driven inflation. Despite these hawkish pressures, the AUD has managed to trade toward new highs near 0.7000 against the USD, supported by risk-on flows in specific crosses and a bullish near-term technical structure. However, the currency remains sensitive to the 6.6% decline in the South Korean Kospi and broader Asian equity weakness stemming from regional geopolitical instability.

sources.title: Dollar jumps on renewed Middle East attacks, Hormuz closure - CNBC
Fourth Australian interest rate rise more likely if Trump’s Iran conflict not resolved within a week - The Guardian

CAD.catalyst: BoC 2.25%. Governor Tiff Macklem noted in the post-meeting press conference that Q2 expansion looks solid, supported by a June jobs report that saw 18,200 positions added, nearly double the expected 10,000. USD/CAD has recently retraced from the 1.4250 resistance level toward 1.4130, though it continues to exhibit a significant correlation break from WTI oil prices. Despite oil trading at $80.1, the Loonie remains weighed down by a -150bp carry disadvantage against the USD and heavy net short positioning among large speculators.

sources.title: Gold holding steady as Bank of Canada leaves rates unchanged - KITCO

CHF.catalyst: The Swiss Franc is currently holding onto recent losses as the U.S. dollar recovers on the back of Middle East tensions and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Recent SNB minutes revealed that while officials acknowledge rising inflation risks, they see no immediate need for policy action, which has dampened demand for the Franc as a proactive hedge. Fed 3.75%. Traders are currently identifying actionable patterns in USD/CHF as it tests recent highs, driven by the recovery in U.S. real rates.

NZD.catalyst: The New Zealand Dollar has emerged as a G10 leader, holding gains around 0.5850 despite a 125bp nominal carry disadvantage against the Federal Reserve. This outperformance is fueled by a hawkish RBNZ stance and a significant short-squeeze risk, as COT data shows leveraged funds holding an extreme net short position of 27,675 contracts. The currency found additional support as the USD softened ahead of Kevin Warsh’s testimony and recent U.S. CPI data. While record low bets were recently reported, the RBNZ's 2.5% policy rate and the market's positioning extreme have created a floor for the Kiwi amid the broader geopolitical volatility.

sources.title: Fragile yen set for weekly decline as intervention risks mount - CNBC

NOK.catalyst: The Norwegian Krone is currently trading in a range-bound fashion as market participants await the release of Norsk Hydro’s second-quarter 2026 results on July 22. While the currency typically benefits from rising energy prices, the current geopolitical premium in oil is being offset by the broader flight to the U.S. dollar and regional European growth concerns. Domestic data remains sparse this week, leaving the Krone sensitive to shifts in Brent crude and the evolving conflict in the Persian Gulf. Without fresh central bank catalysts from Norges Bank, the currency is largely tracking global risk sentiment and the performance of its G10 beta peers.

sources.title: Norsk Hydro: Reminder - Invitation to Hydro's second quarter results 2026 - markets.businessinsider.com

SEK.catalyst: The Swedish Krona remains range-bound pending fresh domestic catalysts, as the market focuses on larger G10 themes including the U.S.-Iran conflict and Fed policy shifts. There have been no major data releases or Riksbank communications this week to drive independent price action, leaving SEK to trade as a proxy for European growth and global risk appetite. The currency is currently caught between the tailwinds of higher global yields and the headwinds of a slowing Eurozone industrial sector. Consequently, SEK is expected to maintain its current levels against the Euro and Dollar until the next round of Swedish inflation or manufacturing data provides a new directional lead.
